Notes/Context: Normal is <120/<80. Population averages vary by age. Hypertension Stage 1 is defined at 80-89 mmHg. 2
Resting rate is influenced by fitness; athletes may have rates as low as 40 bpm. Women's resting rates are often 5-10 bpm higher than men's. 3
Also known as Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP). A value <60-65 mmHg raises concern for inadequate organ perfusion. 2
SpO2. Values may be lower in older adults or those with chronic lung disease. A value <92% requires urgent attention. 32
Normal range for adults at rest. Can be slightly higher in older adults. 7
Normal is <120/<80. Population averages vary by age. Hypertension Stage 1 is defined at 130-139 mmHg. 2
Average is ~97.9°F (36.6°C). Ranges vary by measurement site and time of day. 44
Therapeutic target. Set to achieve target oxygen saturation, ideally <60% to avoid toxicity. 51
Therapeutic target. Set to achieve target oxygen saturation, ideally <60% to avoid toxicity. 51
Monitored value, not a set range. Influenced by airway resistance and lung compliance. 51
Therapeutic target. Goal is <30 cmH2O to prevent lung injury (barotrauma). 51
Therapeutic target. Typically set at ≥5 cmH2O to prevent alveolar collapse. 51
Therapeutic target. Typically set at ≥5 cmH2O to prevent alveolar collapse. 51
Therapeutic target. May be increased up to 35 to maintain adequate ventilation with low tidal volumes. 51
Monitored value. Corresponds to the set tidal volume.
Therapeutic target based on Ideal Body Weight (IBW) for lung protection. 51
Monitored value. Volume of patient-triggered breaths.
Cardiac output indexed to body surface area. 28
Thermodilution (TD) is a common measurement method, often preferred over estimated Fick. 28
Fick method is another way to measure cardiac output. Direct Fick is the gold standard but rarely used. Estimated Fick has limitations. 54
Some sources cite 0-8 mmHg. Reflects right ventricular preload. 28
Clinical scoring system. A score ≤8 indicates severe impairment. Not a lab value. 60
Mean Pulmonary Artery Pressure (MPAP). Reflects right ventricular afterload. 28
Pulmonary Artery Systolic Pressure (PASP). 28
Also known as PCWP or PAWP. Reflects left ventricular preload. 28
A measure of left ventricular afterload. 28
Calculated value ([Na+]−([Cl−]+[HCO3−​])). Should be corrected for low albumin. 63
Also reported as CO2. Venous/serum value. Primary metabolic buffer. 68
Refers to serum bicarbonate in a metabolic panel. 68
This typically refers to End-Tidal CO2 (EtCO2), which approximates arterial PCO2 in healthy individuals. 75
Resting venous level. Levels >4 mmol/L define lactic acidosis. 81
Same as lactate. Resting venous level. Levels >4 mmol/L define lactic acidosis. 81
Arterial PCO2 (PaCO2). Primary indicator of alveolar ventilation. 28
Arterial PO2 (PaO2). Measures oxygen dissolved in arterial blood. 28
Arterial pH. A measure of blood acidity. 28
Mixed Venous pO2. Reflects overall balance of oxygen delivery and tissue consumption. 84
BUN. Waste product of protein metabolism cleared by kidneys. 68
Total Calcium. Must be interpreted in the context of albumin levels. 68
Measures the physiologically active form of calcium; preferred in critically ill patients. 68
Waste product of muscle metabolism; more specific marker of kidney function than BUN. 68
Fasting glucose. Primary energy source for cells. 68
Major intracellular cation, critical for cardiac function. 68
Same as Potassium. 68
Major extracellular cation, critical for fluid balance. 68
Major extracellular anion. 68
Hct. Percentage of blood volume occupied by red blood cells. 68
Hgb. Oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells. 68
Cells responsible for blood clotting. 68
RBC. Count of red blood cells. 68
WBC. Total count of all white blood cells. 68
Measures a key clotting protein. 94
PTT/aPTT. Measures intrinsic pathway. Lab-dependent. 68
INR. Standardized ratio for non-anticoagulated patients. Therapeutic targets vary. 68
PT. Measures extrinsic pathway. Lab-dependent; INR is the standardized value. 68
ALT. Liver enzyme. Some guidelines suggest stricter upper limits for higher sensitivity. 68
Main protein in plasma, synthesized by the liver. 68
ALP. Liver and bone enzyme. 68
AST. Liver and muscle enzyme. 68
Total Bilirubin. Product of heme breakdown, processed by the liver. 68
Total Cholesterol. Desirable target for cardiovascular risk reduction. 68
"Good" cholesterol. Higher levels (>60) are protective. 68
"Bad" cholesterol. Optimal target. Lower targets (<70) for high-risk patients. 68
LDH. Non-specific marker of tissue damage. 68
Essential mineral and enzyme cofactor. 68
Also known as Phosphorous. Essential for bone health and cellular energy. 68
Same as Phosphate. 68
Measures the total amount of protein in the blood. 68
Conventional assay. High-sensitivity assays have different, lower cutoffs. 4
Conventional assay. High-sensitivity assays have different, lower cutoffs. 4
Absolute count. More clinically relevant than percentage. 90
Absolute count. More clinically relevant than percentage. 90
Absolute count. More clinically relevant than percentage. 90
Absolute lymphocyte count is the clinically preferred metric.
MCH. Average amount of hemoglobin per red blood cell. 68
MCHC. Average concentration of hemoglobin inside a red blood cell. 68
MCV. Average size of red blood cells. 68
Absolute count. More clinically relevant than percentage. 90
Absolute count. More clinically relevant than percentage. 90
Not a "normal" value. Used to calculate Serum-Ascites Albumin Gradient (SAAG) to determine cause of effusion. 121
Not a "normal" value. Used in Light's Criteria to classify pleural effusions. 124
Albumin-to-Creatinine Ratio (ACR). A general cutoff of <30 mg/g is considered normal. 67
24-hour collection. Clinical cutoffs for kidney stone risk; highly diet-dependent. 67
24-hour collection. Reflects dietary intake and volume status. 67
Not normally present. High levels can indicate bladder perforation. 122
Term is too generic. Reference range depends on the specific body fluid (e.g., pleural, ascitic).
Not a standard test for pleural fluid analysis.
24-hour collection. Reflects muscle mass and kidney function. 67
Anthropometric measurement. Used to calculate BMI, BSA, and Ideal Body Weight (IBW).
Not a "normal" value. Used in Light's Criteria. Transudates typically have LDH <200 IU/L. 124
Not a "normal" value. Predominance helps determine the cause of the effusion. 121
Also called reactive lymphocytes. Presence of >10-20% is a classic finding in viral infections like mononucleosis. 142
Same as Atypical Lymphocytes. CSL is likely a lab-specific term. 142
Term is too generic. Reference range depends on the specific body fluid (e.g., CSF, pleural).
Not a "normal" value. Predominance helps determine the cause of the effusion (e.g., TB, malignancy). 124
Same as Monocytes (absolute). CSL is likely a lab-specific term. 90
PVR. Volume of urine remaining in the bladder after urination. >100-200 mL is considered abnormal. 150
Cerebrospinal Fluid. Normally acellular. 152
Normally absent. A count >10,000 cells/µL is considered hemorrhagic. 122
Normally absent. A count >100,000 cells/µL suggests hemothorax. 124
Number of red blood cells per high-power field in microscopic analysis. 86
24-hour collection. Measures total protein excreted over 24 hours. 67
Anthropometric measurement. Used to calculate BMI and BSA.
Number of white blood cells per high-power field in microscopic analysis. 86
Measures the acidity or alkalinity of urine. 68
